nativeRange.habitat: Subtidal lagoon and seaward reefs at roughly 2–30 m, typically sheltering in crevices, ledges, and among branching corals such as Acropora and Pocillopora

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting most meaty marine foods in captivity: mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped krill, shrimp, squid and clam, plus quality marine pellets and flakes. Generally advised to offer 2–3 small meals per day and to include vitamin-rich foods (e.g., enriched mysis, vitamin-soaked frozens, marine-algae preparations) to maintain the bright yellow coloration of yellow color-phase specimens.

summary: A small but assertive Indo-Pacific dottyback that ranges naturally from bright yellow to dark brown. Generally considered hardy and beginner-friendly, but tends to be territorial and has been known to eat ornamental shrimp; best kept singly.
careNotes:
The Yellow Dottyback is a color form of Pseudochromis fuscus, a polymorphic Indo-Pacific reef predator whose wild specimens are commonly traded under several names — Dusky Dottyback, Brown Dottyback, Golden Dottyback, and Musky Dottyback — depending on the dominant color of the individual. Yellow-phase specimens are the most commonly traded form in the aquarium hobby. Diet has been shown to influence the intensity of the yellow color phase, so a varied, vitamin-rich feeding regimen is generally advised to keep yellow individuals from fading toward brown over time.

The species is generally considered hardy and one of the more straightforward saltwater fish for new reefkeepers — it tolerates a typical reef-tank parameter window and readily accepts prepared and frozen marine foods. It spends most of its time darting between rockwork in the mid water column, so plenty of caves, overhangs, and branching structure are generally advised.

Temperament is the most important caveat. P. fuscus is widely considered semi-aggressive to aggressive and unusually bold for its size — it tends to defend a small home territory in the rockwork and bully smaller, more timid fish. Conspecific aggression is reported as high, so it is best kept one per tank unless the system is very large with multiple discrete territories. The species is a genuine micro-predator in the wild (roughly 10% of piscivore biomass on some reefs), and has been known to consume small ornamental shrimp, mobile micro-inverts, and very small fish; a tank combining this species with a dedicated shrimp display is not generally advised. Reef compatibility with corals and sessile invertebrates is generally considered good.

Like many dottybacks, the species is reported to be capable of sex change (female to male), and pairs occasionally form in the trade, though aquarists typically keep singles. A well-sealed lid is generally advised, as dottybacks have been known to jump from open-topped tanks.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to spawn in captivity and is commercially captive-bred. Cave-spawning species: the male prepares a hiding site and courts a female, who deposits a small egg ball inside the prepared cave. The male tends the eggs until hatching. Captive-bred specimens are increasingly available and tend to be more even-tempered toward tankmates than wild-caught fish.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for minimum tank (30 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.021–1.025, dKH 8–12), care level (Easy), temperament (Semi-aggressive), reef compatibility (with caution — will eat small ornamental shrimp), diet (carnivore meaty foods), max size (4 in / ~10.2 cm), and the vitamin-rich diet note for color retention. The Reef2Reef secondary URL supplied by the discovery agent returned HTTP 403, so cross-verification was performed against FishBase and Wikipedia (documented below).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked confirmed scientific name Pseudochromis fuscus Müller & Troschel 1849, family Pseudochromidae, max length 10.0 cm TL, depth range 2–30 m, native distribution (Sri Lanka east to Vanuatu, Hong Kong/Ryukyus south to Western Australia and southern GBR), reef-associated habitat in branching corals (Acropora, Pocillopora), and wild temperature range 25.3–29°C. IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used as fallback secondary because the discovery-supplied Reef2Reef thread URL returned HTTP 403. Confirmed common names (Brown/Golden/Musky/Dusky Dottyback), ~10 cm max size, Indo-Pacific distribution, color polymorphism from bright yellow to dark brown (also orange/pink/gray), aggressive piscivore comprising up to ~10% of piscivore biomass on some reefs, diet-driven color intensity, and the species' role as a micro-predator that selects rare and longer-bodied prey.

fish.breedingNotes: The Yellow Dottyback is a cave-spawning species. The male prepares a hiding site and courts a female, who deposits a small egg ball inside; the male then tends the eggs until they hatch. Captive-bred specimens are commercially available and tend to be more even-tempered toward tankmates than wild-caught fish.
